title: $E_\infty^{1,2}$-type Lieb-Schultz-Mattis anomalies, deconfined quantum critical points, and non-invertible symmetry breaking
authors: Hao-Ran Zhang, Hanlin Lin, Shuo Yang, Qing-Rui Wang
abstract: We study deconfined quantum critical points (DQCP) associated with Lieb-Schultz-Mattis (LSM) anomalies in one-dimensional spin chains. Our starting point is a structural characterization of the LSM anomaly in the Lyndon-Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence: $ω_{\mathrm{LSM}}\in E_\infty^{1,2}= H^1(\mathbb Z_{\mathrm{trans}},H^2(G_{\mathrm{int}},\mathrm{U}(1)))\subseteq H^3(G_{\mathrm{int}}\rtimes_ρ\mathbb Z_{\mathrm{trans}},\mathrm{U}(1))$. Physically, this class decorates a translation defect with a projective representation of the internal symmetry $G_\mathrm{int}$. We show that gauging the internal symmetry in the presence of an $E_\infty^{1,2}$-type anomaly necessarily produces a non-invertible dual symmetry. This gives a general mechanism for type-II DQCP: in contrast to type-I examples with $E_\infty^{2,1}$-type anomalies which are dual to ordinary group-like symmetry breaking, type-II transitions are dual to spontaneous breaking of a non-invertible symmetry. We illustrate the mechanism using a spin-$1/2$ chain with an anomalous $D_8$ LSM symmetry. We construct a dimer-to-ferromagnet DQCP candidate, provide numerical evidence for a critical theory with central charge $c\approx 1$, and show, using both category theory and explicit lattice constructions, that gauging the internal symmetry yields the non-invertible $\mathrm{Rep}(H_8)$ dual symmetry.
summary: The paper establishes a general mechanism showing that specific types of Lieb-Schultz-Mattis anomalies ($E_{1,2}^{\infty}$) dictate that the resulting deconfined quantum critical points must be dual to the spontaneous breaking of a non-invertible symmetry. This is demonstrated using 1D spin chains and provides a powerful mathematical tool for classifying exotic quantum phase transitions.
main_problem: The study characterizes Deconfined Quantum Critical Points (DQCPs) associated with Lieb-Schultz-Mattis (LSM) anomalies in 1D spin chains, focusing on the implications of an $E_\infty^{1,2}$-type anomaly.
main_result: An $E_{1,2}^{\infty}$-type LSM anomaly necessarily leads to a non-invertible dual symmetry upon gauging the internal symmetry, defining 'type-II' DQCPs distinct from type-I transitions.
method: The analysis uses advanced mathematical tools, including the Lyndon-Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence and category theory, to structurally characterize anomalies and gauge symmetries.
model_or_system: The primary system is one-dimensional spin chains. A specific example analyzed is a dimer-to-ferromagnet DQCP candidate using a spin-1/2 chain with an anomalous $D_8$ LSM symmetry.
key_observables: The type of anomaly ($E_{1,2}^{\infty}$ vs $E_{2,1}^{\infty}$) and the nature of the dual symmetry (invertible group symmetry vs non-invertible fusion-category symmetry).
important_parameters: Central charge $c \approx 1$; the structure of the internal symmetry group ($D_8$); and the anomaly class $\omega_{LSM} \in E_\infty^{1,2}$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on structural characterizations within the LHS spectral sequence framework and assumes Abelian normal subgroups for certain categorical simplifications.
figures_summary: Figure 1 summarizes that a nontrivial $E_{1,2}^{\infty}$ component causes non-invertibility in the dual symmetry. Figure 2 illustrates how $E_{1,2}^{\infty}$ leads to an A-SPT decorating the wall, resulting in a non-invertible dual defect.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by mathematically characterizing the LSM anomaly using the LHS spectral sequence, establishing the general link between $E_{1,2}^{\infty}$-anomalies and non-invertible symmetries, and then illustrating this mechanism with explicit lattice constructions and numerical evidence for a specific DQCP candidate.
why_interesting: This work provides a deep mathematical framework connecting fundamental symmetry constraints (LSM) to the nature of quantum critical points via advanced concepts like non-invertible symmetries, which are crucial for understanding exotic topological phases in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Effect of LHS anomaly components under gauging an Abelian normal subgroup A of G. The key result is that a nontrivial E1,2 ∞component produces projective A-sectors and hence a non-invertible dual symmetry, whereas E3,0 ∞and E2,1 ∞data keep the dual symmetry invertible.

FIG. 2: Decorated-domain-wall representatives for a 2+1D bulk SPT with 1 →A →G →Q →1. Left: an E2,1 ∞ decoration puts A charges on Q-wall junctions, so gauging A keeps the dual symmetry invertible. Right: an E1,2 ∞ decoration puts a 1+1D A-SPT on each Q wall; its boundary endpoint carries a projective A representation, which becomes a non-invertible dual defect after gauging A.

FIG. 3: The peak of the MPS correlation length ξ(χ) grows with increasing bond dimension χ, indicating a continuous transition between the dimer phase near λ = 0 and the zFM phase at large positive λ. As shown in Fig. 4, the peak height follows a power-law scaling with χ.

FIG. 4: The MPS correlation length ξ(χ) exhibits power-law scaling with χ in the critical regime. In the insets, the peak value ξ[χ, λc(χ)] is plotted as a function of χ, from which the finite-entanglement exponent κ is extracted. The estimate from the dimer ansatz is shown in the left panel, while that from the zFM ansatz is shown in the right panel.

FIG. 5: The MPS entanglement entropy S(χ) is plotted as a function of the logarithm of the MPS correlation length ξ(χ) at the pseudocritical points λc(χ). The linear dependence is consistent with entropy scaling S(χ) ∼c
